# **What about China? A guide to the details of treaty compliance for AI Governance**

When thinking about how to make AI go well, many of the plans that have been proposed rely on a systematic attempt at global AI governance. This includes attempts at [global coordination schemes](https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/g7-hiroshima-process-on-generative-artificial-intelligence-ai_bf3c0c60-en.html), [global pauses](https://pauseai.info/)/[moratoria](https://joecarlsmith.substack.com/p/on-restraining-ai-development-for), [non-proliferation treaties](https://www.nationalsecurity.ai/chapter/nonproliferation) or [cooperative development](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/jwhcXmigv2LTrbBiB/success-without-dignity-a-nearcasting-story-of-avoiding%20) proposals. 

This is likely to take a form that looks like a global treaty. These could be bilateral, or between a few parties. They could be within existing institutions like the EU AI Act or G7 Hiroshima process, or would need new institutions. They could be realistic extensions of existing agreements, or bolder “moonshots”, like those proposed by [MIRI](https://www.lesswrong.com/posts/FA6M8MeQuQJxZyzeq/new-report-an-international-agreement-to-prevent-the) or [A Narrow Path](https://www.narrowpath.co/fr/). 

But they have one thing in common \- China has to have a central role, probably not just as a signatory but as a central participant and “rule-maker” in the process. 

This correctly causes us all to pause. China is a genuinely hard-to-read country with whom, at best, the US and Europe have shaky relations with. It’s also a nondemocratic country that is seen by many as a dishonest and untrustworthy international actor. 

The "What about China?" question seems to be so triggering, causing some to dismiss any possibility of an agreement, that it recently led Scott Alexander to fall from his usually flawless style to write [his worst blogpost ever](https://www.astralcodexten.com/p/every-debate-on-pausing-ai) critiquing their position. 

But even removed from 2026 MAGA-driven polarisation, China’s role in international governance proposals still poses a huge and challenging question. In 2025 and 2026, Chinese actors have been playing a more serious role in AI Governance, but I’m yet to see careful consideration of how China would react to various attempts at AI Governance proposals or treaties. From my engagement with people in the EA/rationality space, we aren't generally well-equipped to answer it. Those who *are* in a position to answer with some authority tend to be in positions where it's unwise to state their opinions openly. So I'll have a go. This is the thesis question:

*Can we (the collective "West", I suppose) ever trust the Chinese authorities? Can we ever know if they’re acting sincerely? Can we “make a deal”?*

I'm writing as someone who reads and writes Chinese and with a fair amount of experience in China and writing about China, but I’m not a legal scholar, so please correct any misunderstandings I may have. I've spent a good chunk of time digging into legal scholarship in China and the history of treaty compliance there.

My hope is that this gives readers a better sense of what China's system actually looks like, so that when we think about the workability of international treaties or other governance approaches, we have the right picture in mind.

I'm going to lay this out in a few sections:

1. How treaties actually work  
2. How China & international law works  
3. How treaties enter Chinese law specifically  
4. Case studies of compliance and non-compliance  
5. The patterns

## **1\. How Do Treaties Actually Work?**

Let's start with the basics. International treaties are coordination tools that are used to solve problems that make doing things between nations more difficult. 

There are around 560 major multilateral treaties and hundreds of thousands of bilateral ones. The "international community", often led by a handful of nations or bodies, decides that something needs coordinating. This might be to raise the costs of war (the Geneva Conventions), make trade easier (the World Trade Organisation), signal that we want to stop bad things from happening (human rights treaties), or protect some commons (environmental and ocean treaties). 

\[Add data on treaty types\]

International treaties are not the same as domestic law, and rely on different mechanisms to enforce. Countries, or individuals within countries, who break the terms of a treaty don’t always face direct, centralised punishment. In fact, international treaty bodies often don’t even *expect* countries to follow their agreements, and they can serve more of a symbolic or aspirational purpose.

As such, the [skeptic argues that](https://stratechery.com/2026/anthropic-and-alignment/) “the very concept of “international law” is fake, not because pertinent statutes and agreements don’t exist, but because their effectiveness is ultimately rooted in their enforceability. That, by extension, means there must be an entity to enact such enforcement, with the capability to match, and such an entity does not exist.” 

Whether countries actually do what a treaty says depends massively on the terms, the process, and the conditions under which they were signed. For example, France signing & ratifying a human rights treaty immediately results in French courts being able to enforce it as law, while North Korea signing the same treaty is just LARPing at being a serious country. 

Treaties work through several mechanisms. Some resemble (and work through) domestic law \- treaty participants agree to punish rule-breakers and allow law-abiding parties to function more efficiently. But they also work in subtler ways, such as building credibility between parties, reducing uncertainty in trade, helping build reciprocity over time, lowering transaction costs by establishing shared frameworks, and creating focal points that make future coordination easier.

The process itself typically looks like this:

1. **Participation:** Deciding that a treaty should happen, and about who is at the table in the first place  
2. **Preparation and negotiation:** Representatives work out the terms before anyone puts pen to paper  
3. **Signing:** Someone representing the country \- usually the executive \- formally agrees to enter into the agreement  
4. **Ratification:** A country, often through a different body than the one that signed, formally agrees to be bound  
5. **Implementation:** The treaty is interpreted and applied by state administration or courts \- this often means making new laws

The interesting action happens in the gaps between these stages, and different countries have very different bottlenecks at different points.

The **participation gap** concerns who is at the table, and who is a rule-maker/rule-taker. Whether a process is between two parties or open to all parties makes an enormous difference to what gets agreed. (In fact, bilateral treaties are significantly more likely to be effective). This makes sense \- negotiations involving Iran and Somalia look very different from those involving only North American and European countries. Who controls the initial draft also matters \- there is good evidence (find) that whichever country or secretariat gets the first text written down has a disproportionate influence on the final outcome, since negotiations tend to anchor to whatever is already on the page.

The **negotiation-to-signing gap** depends heavily on what comes later. If negotiators know their country will be automatically bound by what they agree, they will be very careful to hash out terms with other ministers from their own government before putting their demands down at the negotiating table.. Robert Putnam calls this a “two-level game” \- if they know ratification requires a separate political fight at home, they may move faster internationally but face trouble later. (Add veto points here)

The **signing-to-ratification gap** happens when different parts of government are responsible for each step. In a *monist* system like France, once a treaty is ratified it is essentially law \- the administration applies it and courts can refer to it directly. In *dualist* systems like the UK or US, the treaty must be separately written into domestic law before courts can enforce it. The US [requires a two-thirds Senate majority for ratification](https://www.senate.gov/about/powers-procedures/treaties.htm#:~:text=The%20United%20States%20Constitution%20provides,become%20part%20of%20international%20law.), which, owing to crazy levels of political polarisation, is a serious bottleneck \- called a Veto point by George Tsebelis. As a result, the US has ratified roughly half the environmental treaties its European neighbours have ([link](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/political-science/articles/10.3389/fpos.2024.1388191/full)). China is different: there is no meaningful separation between the actors who sign and those who ratify, so the political decision is made upstream during negotiation. Ratification is more of a rubber stamp, typically completed within a single session of the [NPC Standing Committee](https://english.court.gov.cn/), around two to four months.

The **ratification-to-implementation** gap is where things get most interesting for this piece, and it’s where the classic “compliance gap” comes up. Some countries, like France, automatically apply parts of a ratified treaty through their courts and administration. In others, particularly China, the big question is whether a ratified treaty actually ends up changing anything at all \- and if so, in what form. Implementation can take two broad routes: administrative action, where relevant government departments do stuff differently, or legislative incorporation, where the treaty obligations are added into domestic law. The choice between these routes matters both for how legible the obligations become and for how they can be enforced. (Add veto points here)

## **2\. How Does China Engage With International Law?**

If you follow coverage of stuff like [China intellectual property rights](https://www.trade.gov/country-commercial-guides/china-protecting-intellectual-property), [Xinjiang human rights abuses](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Persecution_of_Uyghurs_in_China), [erosion of freedoms in Hong Kong,](https://www.cfr.org/backgrounders/hong-kong-freedoms-democracy-protests-china-crackdown) and [naval law violations](https://www.lowyinstitute.org/the-interpreter/how-china-bending-rules-south-china-sea), it's easy to slip into "you can’t do business with China" cynicism. China is impossible to engage with seriously because it lacks democratic representation, independent courts and a free media etc. 

And, of course, China isn't Sweden. 

Some countries are broadly content with the rules of the international order, and engage in good faith with the UN, the WTO, the international court of human rights etc. China is selective with how it engages internationally, and has some misalignment with the processes and norms of international agreements and the international community. While public speeches are careful to distinguish the international community (good) and U.S. hegemony (bad), leaked [internal party documents](https://chinadigitaltimes.net/space/%E5%85%B3%E4%BA%8E%E5%BD%93%E5%89%8D%E6%84%8F%E8%AF%86%E5%BD%A2%E6%80%81%E9%A2%86%E5%9F%9F%E6%83%85%E5%86%B5%E7%9A%84%E9%80%9A%E6%8A%A5) openly reject universal values that define a lot of the international order. China’s leaders also (often justifiably) think that some US-led parts of the international order are deliberately acting against the interests of countries like China. As such, they are “functionalist” in their views on international law ([EIAS](https://www.eias.org/policy-briefs/how-china-approaches-international-law-implications-for-europe/)), rather than “normative”, meaning that they uphold international law to the extent that it serves a function, but do not have a moral commitment to the rules or the underlying ideology.

But China isn’t North Korea. It engages proactively with both democracies and dictatorships on the international scene. When China is accused of breaching its international commitments, it’s usually related to national security interests and territorial sovereignty, such as in Hong Kong and the South China Sea. In other areas, such as human rights and climate change agreements, China is typically careful to limit its commitments so that it does not formally breach them (such as [not ratifying the International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights (ICCPR)](https://www.hrw.org/news/2013/10/08/china-ratify-key-international-human-rights-treaty)) . It also has a well-functioning administrative system that enables Chinese administrators to competently and transparently govern a bunch of international treaties. Things like licensing, trade regulation, customs, monitoring of chemical and nuclear facilities, etc. all of this can work at scale. To uphold international law, China has courts with genuine technical competence, in commercial and intellectual property law, as well as responsible parties in scientific fields. Thousands of thoroughly uninteresting cases are adjudicated professionally every year. 

However, these institutions aren't independent in the way we expect in democratic countries. In most European countries, we have an independent civil service and court systems that administers rules and adjudicates decisions in ways that frequently go against the leadership or the ruling political party. Politicians often grumble about treaties their predecessors signed, but their courts keep enforcing them regardless. This doesn't happen in China. Unlike countries like Russia with a fig-leaf of democratic representation, in China there isn't even the pretence that legal institutions and the civil service are independent from the party-state. In China, the same political leadership that decides to sign a treaty also has the ultimate control over the institutions that implement it. 

This means that implementation can be more efficient when the leadership wants it--there's no Senate or Supreme Court to get in the way. But as soon as a treaty goes up against the party’s objectives, it tumbles like a paper-tiger.

The key question for international treaties is whether you can design obligations that remain meaningful even within a system without these separations of power, and where political objectives can override legal and administrative norms.

## **3\.  How does a treaty actually enter Chinese law**

When countries sign and ratify international treaties, the first question is what kind of domestic action the commitment actually requires. 

So you might get a treaty obligation that says:

1. China commits to submitting an annual report to an international AI safety body on domestic AI incidents above a certain severity threshold.

And another that says:

2. China commits to ensuring no frontier model is deployed without prior safety evaluation by a designated authority.

The first action is a **purely administrative obligation**. It can simply be managed by getting the CAC (Cyberspace Administration of China) to compile and transmit data. As domestic reporting obligations already require companies to report to the CAC, the international commitment only affects administrative actors.

The second action is a **legislative obligation**. It actually requires China to pass legislation that binds AI developers, creates an evaluation process that can block the release of models, and creates the mechanisms for punishment if a model is released without these precautions.  

Administrative obligations have some problems \- without any official legislative change, compliance depends on the discretion of a ministry and, in China in particular, is vulnerable to shifts with political winds, especially in the case of international tensions.

Legislative obligations have other problems. When a treaty needs to bind private citizens and courts to new rules forcing companies to meet certain standards, or creating liability for harm, the state has to do something it may have no current legal authority to do. These obligations have to pass through the domestic legislative system before they can actually affect domestic actors.

Here, the structure of a country's legal system affects how the treaty obligation is treated. 

First, a quick description of the system. For a treaty to be recognised in China, the [National People's Congress Standing Committee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Standing_Committee_of_the_National_People%27s_Congress) \- a body of around 160 people who serve as the permanent working arm of China's legislature (not to be confused with the smaller [*Politburo* Standing Committee](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Politburo_Standing_Committee_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party)) \- has to ratify it. This is not a political process, as the treaty text has already been approved by the negotiators and the party leaders.

It's then published (in Chinese, naturally) in the *Gazette of the National People's Congress Standing Committee* (全国人民代表大会常务委员会公报) and on the NPC website. This is the main step of ratification and it doesn't require any further domestic approval.

![][image1]

Then it can work in a couple of ways. In the past, it was dependent on **reference norms**; essentially, rules that tell Chinese courts "look at this treaty when deciding cases." The most famous example was Article 142(2) of the General Principles of Civil Law (1986), which said something to the effect of:

"If any international treaty concluded or acceded to by the People's Republic of China contains provisions differing from those in the civil laws of the PRC, the provisions of the international treaty shall apply, unless … China has announced reservations."

This meant that some treaties have ended up being directly implemented. When a dispute emerged, Chinese courts could simply say "China agreed to this treaty, so it takes precedence." So, like the French system, once ratified, a treaty is considered law and courts can refer to it. [This piece](https://danielturpqc.org/upload/2015findoc/XUE_et_JIN-_International_Treaties_in_China.pdf) by a former Chinese law insider, describes the process, and the exceptions, in a lot of detail.

If everything worked like this, it would be relatively straightforward to guarantee that Chinese courts would at least *try* to enforce international obligations. But it didn't last. 

In 2002, the Supreme People's Court issued a judicial interpretation ([关于审理国际贸易行政案件若干问题的规定](http://gongbao.court.gov.cn/Details/a93ac8e91546c57b86aaf42972195f.html)) that explicitly told courts: when trying international trade administrative cases, you’ve got to apply Chinese laws, administrative regulations, and local regulations. In 2020, Article 142(2) itself \- the original reference norm that had enabled direct treaty application \- was abolished when China launched its new Civil Code. It wasn't replaced with anything equivalent. This all meant that China’s treaty governance was moving more towards a “transformation” model, rather than adopting treaties wholesale. Now, China increasingly needs to *transform* the treaty into a Chinese-law-compatible language.

## How does this matter?

Now we actually have to transform treaty obligations into Chinese law, we have a few problems. There’s no internationally recognised process whereby this is monitored, so any obligation is between Chinese actors. This gives a lot of leeway for treaty obligations to be diluted or ignored. There are a couple of important points here:

The first question is how precisely an obligation is specified \- if you ask China to fulfill a binary and technical obligation to criminalise export of a product with certain characteristics (e.g. nuclear material), this is likely to be translated directly into domestic law, because there’s little room for interpretation. But obligations with ambiguity might say stuff like "make efforts to," "take appropriate measures," "progressively implement" \- give the drafting ministry enormous discretion. In this case, what emerges in domestic law may look quite different from what the treaty intended. 

The second question is who is responsible for enforcement. Some treaties designate a specific ministry or body to monitor compliance; others leave this entirely to domestic discretion. Where a centralised authority, such as a national office, is given explicit responsibility, obligations tend to be tracked and reported on. Where responsibility is diffuse there can be “buck-passing” (see Huang Yanzhong’s books on [health](https://www.cfr.org/books/governing-health-contemporary-china) and [the environment](https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/toxic-politics/4ABF95F247347927715364ADF5A9A94D)), or it can be imprecisely defined such that no-one takes ownership. 

These two questions \- how precisely is the obligation specified, and who is concretely responsible for enforcing it \- are useful lenses for predicting whether a commitment will survive the journey from treaty text into Chinese domestic practice. A precise obligation with a clear enforcement body is far more likely to generate real-world compliance, regardless of how the political winds shift.

The main takeaway from this is that there is no automatic pathway from signing a treaty to having it enforced in China. The text has to be translated into domestic law, the obligation has to be specific enough to survive that translation, and someone has to be made responsible for checking that it was followed. For AI governance, where obligations are likely to be technically complex, politically sensitive, and genuinely novel , each of those steps presents its own serious difficulty.

## **4\. Case Studies**

Let's look at some real examples of how China has or hasn't adhered to international treaties it has signed in the past. There are careful papers on how effectively treaties are implemented globally and in China; however, my sense is that each case is specific enough, and the sample size of major international treaties small enough, that we can’t draw any strong systematic conclusions. However, there seem to be some logical narratives as to how some treaties have worked more or less well.

### **The Chemical Weapons Convention (CWC) (In this section I want to** 

Negotiated in the late Cold War period (1990–1992) and entering into force in 1997, the CWC was driven by the shared interest of major powers in eliminating an already-stigmatised class of weapons. China signed in 1993 and ratified in 1997, playing a largely cooperative role \- this was partly shaped by its interest in addressing Japanese chemical weapons left on its territory.  (What happened domestically in China? Transformation?)

![][image2]

Ultimately, the CWC is a collective-action problem: everyone wants to avoid a world where chemical weapons are freely used, but each party would rather the others bore the costs of disarmament, and the indignity of frequent testing and infringement of sovereignty. It actually requires letting independent regulators *into China* to inspect chemical production facilities. This continues to be a genuine success, with over 500 inspections conducted in China since 1997, including regular monitoring of both civilian chemical facilities and sites containing Japanese abandoned chemical weapons.

TAnd China had inherent incentives to participate; Japan had left behind chemical weapons on Chinese soil, and the CWC created a framework for their destruction.

This one seems to be working. China accepts regular inspections by the Organisation for the Prohibition of Chemical Weapons and has built domestic regulatory infrastructure around it. It’s worth noting that this is not a treaty that everyone signs up to. North Korea, which is estimated to hold 2,500 to 5,000 tonnes of chemical weapons, is not a party to the CWC, and this choice symbolically puts further distance between the DPRK and the international order. 

The treaty works in part because the major players are in it, but also because it’s demanding enough that not anyone can join and dilute the effectiveness, or cheapen the currency.

### **The Montreal Protocol**

The Montreal Protocol is the treaty that optimists about international governance love citing. It phased out ozone-depleting chemicals, achieved universal ratification, and actually worked. China, as the world's largest producer and consumer of these chemicals, is one of the core reasons for its success; without Chinese compliance, the treaty would have been largely decorative.

China's CFC phase-out was driven by direct, centralised state action. The government shut down production facilities; the last six CFC plants were closed on 1 July 2007, two and a half years ahead of schedule. This was a binary, top-down intervention: factories were either open or closed, and the state was able and willing to close them.

Two incentives made this politically viable. First, the Multilateral Fund paid for the transition; China received hundreds of millions of dollars across successive phases (e.g. [US$440 million for the fourth ozone-depleting substance project](https://www.worldbank.org/en/results/2014/04/11/world-bank-supports-china-in-phasing-out-ozone-depleting-substances), and a further [$385 million for the hydrochlorofluorocarbon (HCFC) phase](https://sdg.iisd.org/news/multilateral-fund-commits-us385-million-for-hcfc-reduction/)). Second, the Protocol's trade provisions meant that non-compliance would stop Chinese manufacturers from selling a range of relevant products to global markets. Without requiring any norm-shifts or political decision making, it was a straightforward economic calculus where the costs of compliance were covered and the costs of non-compliance were severe.

Treaty design clearly played a role here \- as [a paper](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1177/1070496502238661) on the topic describes:

“Because it closed major loopholes, the sector-based approach to funding was far more effective than the project-by-project approach, leading to China's ultimate success in meeting the protocol's targets to freeze consumption and production of chlorofluorocarbons by 1999 and halons by 2002.”

So China complied partly because of the inherent characteristics, but the details of treaty design also mattered. 

However, where continuous, distributed monitoring was needed rather than one-off plant closures, things got murkier. China's governance is famously campaign-based; the central government periodically emphasises an issue intensely, then loosens its grip as attention shifts to the next priority. It is also necessarily distributed; you cannot govern the production decisions of thousands of small factories from Beijing, so responsibility falls to provincial authorities whose incentives may not align with international commitments. This seems to have happened with CFCs. In 2018, atmospheric scientists detected that global emissions of CFC-11 had been rising since around 2013, years after the global production ban took effect. Follow-up research traced the source: emissions from eastern China were approximately 7,000 tonnes per year higher in 2014–2017 than in 2008–2012, concentrated around the provinces of Shandong and Hebei, accounting for 40–60% of the global increase. The likely cause was that when the next-generation substitute (HCFC-141b) began to be restricted in 2013, some foam insulation producers reverted to the cheaper, banned CFC-11 rather than transitioning to more expensive legal alternatives. This is dscirbed in an UN report: [CFC-11 emissions traced to Shandong province](https://ozone.unep.org/system/files/documents/SAP-2021-report-on-the-unexpected-emissions-of-CFC-11-1268_en.pdf).

![][image3]

The central government did eventually respond. After the research was published and created international embarrassment, Chinese authorities confirmed that banned substances had been identified during factory inspections. Arrests were made, production facilities were demolished, and by 2019 emissions had fallen sharply back to expected levels. But this self-correction only happened because external atmospheric monitoring caught the problem; left to provincial-level enforcement alone, the cheating continued undetected for years.

The centralised, legible pathway succeeded. Binary obligations (close these factories, now\!), combined with direct financial incentives and trade consequences, meant that China was more than willing to take the initiative. But the distributed, ambiguous pathway ran into trouble. When enforcement depended on provincial authorities monitoring the ongoing production choices of small manufacturers, compliance broke down as soon as central attention moved elsewhere.

Two features of this case are worth flagging for the AI governance comparison. First, the Montreal Protocol doesn't require independent regulators to verify adherence at the country level; it was deliberately designed to encourage broad participation (even North Korea signed up) rather than to punish marginal non-compliance. The treaty succeeded at the *system* level despite imperfect country-level compliance; global CFC reductions have been enormous. 

But this system-level success depended on an unusual form of inherent legibility, which doesn’t really work for many obligations. The wonders of atmospheric chemistry allows us to inspect internationally, because the molecules show up in monitoring stations in South Korea and Japan regardless of what you report to Chinese authorities or international inspectors.

### **The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA)**

China’s entry into the International Atomic Energy Agency is probably the most-cited case in the AI governance literature (see Krawic, etc.), and it's generally seen as a strong success.

China signed the central US-China agreement on peaceful uses of nuclear energy in 1985, but the US refused to activate it for roughly a decade due to proliferation concerns \- mainly because China had been selling nuclear material to Iran and Pakistan in the 1990s. However, China has since gone from that position to being generally regarded as a responsible nuclear power, accepting frequent independent inspections, establishing its own regulatory bodies and laws.

How did this happen? The mechanism was bilateral leverage: under US law (via the "123 Agreement"), the US couldn't share civilian nuclear technology unless the partner met non-proliferation standards, including accepting IAEA monitoring. Official IAEA certification was required. Without it, there would be no cooperation and no access to supply chains. China wanted civilian nuclear technology; the US used that desire to force domestic regulatory changes. Eventually, IAEA standards got translated into domestic law through a patchwork of regulations, culminating in the [2017 Nuclear Safety Law](https://radseclexis.stimson.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/01/156.008_China_2017_china_nuclear_safety_law.pdf) (Original Chinese). China stopped exporting nuclear material, and is now seen as a responsible civil nuclear power. 

This is interesting because it shows a pattern where China built domestic regulatory infrastructure gradually, often prompted by IAEA missions, rather than through a single act of adoption. As recently as October 2024, China sent a high-level delegation to IAEA headquarters to discuss its draft Atomic Energy Law, suggesting this process of aligning domestic and international frameworks is still actively ongoing. Importantly, the entire mechanism was administrative regulation, not judicial application.

As recently as October 2024, [China sent](https://www.iaea.org/newscenter/news/strengthening-chinas-national-nuclear-legal-framework-and-future-cooperation-in-nuclear-law) a high-level delegation to IAEA headquarters specifically to discuss nuclear law and aspects of its draft Atomic Energy Law, suggesting this process of aligning domestic and international nuclear governance frameworks is still actively ongoing.

### **The World Trade Organisation (WTO)**

After China’s reform and opening up, China started pivoting from a fully centralised, mostly self-sufficient and state-led economy to an internationalised, export-driven market economy. But in the 1990s, China still had a bunch of obstacles when dealing with other countries \- there was a risk of tariffs or other non-tarriff barriers on Chinese exports; unresolved disputes over intellectual property, services sectors, and agricultural subsidies; and domestically, China was still protecting their industries from foreign competition. Joining the WTO would “hit both targets with one arrow”, and push China’s economy into the modern age.

It’s probably the most complex case here, because it involves a huge range of obligations, from simple tariff rules to complex, process-based commitments that require structural changes to an economy. It’s also been more subject to political shifts than the other treaties, so you can see how robust it is to political headwinds. 

Countries join the WTO to gain access to external markets and to reduce tariff and non-tariff barriers. But membership also serves as a domestic political tool; it gives reformist leaders a way to target rent-seeking industries that profit from protectionism. They want to be able to say "Sorry, my hands are tied" with the force of an international treaty that allows broad economic benefits.

This is exactly what happened in China. Premier Zhu Rongji, viewed by many as one of the most brilliant politicians of his generation, supported joining the WTO to make China's state-led economy more free-market. He (correctly) believed it was weighed down by a load of inefficient, often corrupt state-owned enterprises, that had failed to stay competitive after reform and opening, and that the WTO could be a lever to shape the entire Chinese economy. 

![][image4]

After a messy, decades long process, China acceded at the end of 2001, and the domestic implementation process immediately illustrated the translation process I mentioned earlier. Unlike some previous treaties, the WTO agreements were never published in the NPC Standing Committee Gazette, which meant Chinese courts couldn’t refer to the agreements directly. Instead, it had to write a new set of rules, showing us how international obligations get rewritten as they pass through China’s domestic legislative process. What followed was a massive programme of legislative activity to accommodate WTO provisions across dozens of policy areas. China revised or repealed over 3,000 domestic laws, regulations and administrative orders to achieve WTO compliance. China has often included reservations precluding international arbitration in the international agreements that it has ratified, but this changed under the WTO. 

China complied well with obligations that had clear pass/fail thresholds, and that did not require changing any actual laws. [As reports at the time](https://www.uscc.gov/sites/default/files/Research/china%20compliance%20with%20wto%20obligations%20first%20two%20years.pdf) noted, tariff reductions were implemented on schedule, quantitative restrictions were removed, and specific market access commitments were largely honoured. These are high-legibility obligations; you can check a tariff rate against a schedule and determine compliance in seconds. They also have a built-in enforcement channel: other WTO members can see immediately when a tariff isn't where it should be, and the dispute settlement mechanism provides a route for challenge.

Compliance really broke on subsidies though. Transparency requirements around how local subsidies went chronically unfulfilled \- [a recent USTR report](https://ustr.gov/sites/default/files/USTR%20Report%20on%20China's%20WTO%20Compliance%20\(Final\).pdf) notes that China took 15 years to report its first subsidy through local government, and still consistently under-reports subsidies to the WTO. Intellectual property laws were passed but were basically never enforced in Chinese courts \- the laws existed on paper while being ignored in reality. The broader hope that WTO membership would drive a sustained transition away from the statist direction of the economy never became fully realised.

Under Xi Jinping in particular, China abandoned the market-oriented reform path that some reformers (and the US) were hoping for and continued to try for the best of both worlds. China has since blocked WTO reform proposals that would constrain its industrial policy model. As the US Trade Representative has put it, China still pursues a state-directed, non-market approach to the economy and trade that runs counter to the norms embodied in the WTO. 

**What this tells us**

The WTO case adds a few important considerations.

First, it confirms the legibility pattern. Binary, measurable, centralised obligations (tariff schedules) were implemented reliably; scalar, process-based, distributed obligations (transparency, market reform, IP enforcement) were not. This is consistent with the Montreal Protocol finding, but in a completely different policy domain.

Second, it shows that individual political leadership matters enormously for compliance. Without Zhu Rongji, China's accession would likely not have happened; and without Xi Jinping's turn towards state-led economics, the path China took away from the spirit of the WTO might have looked quite different. Treaties can survive changes of government, but, in a country like China, struggle to survive significant changes in ideology and political economy. For AI governance, this is a warning: any agreement premised on a particular Chinese leadership's priorities is vulnerable to succession.

Third, it highlights a distinctive feature of trade and finance treaties, both sides have a good reason to follow on the transparent, measurable components. Both sides benefit from predictable tariff schedules. Neither side benefits from uncertainty about market access. This makes the legible components of trade agreements more durable than equivalent components in security or environmental treaties, where the incentive structure is more imbalanced. 

[https://ustr.gov/about/policy-offices/press-office/press-releases/2025/january/ustr-releases-annual-report-chinas-wto-compliance](https://ustr.gov/about/policy-offices/press-office/press-releases/2025/january/ustr-releases-annual-report-chinas-wto-compliance) 

### **A Few More**

China has signed human rights treaties but is not bound by them in any meaningful way; they do not enter domestic law.

The Paris Agreement 

With UNCLOS and other maritime laws, China continues to assert military dominance in the South China Sea, ignoring a 2016 tribunal ruling that went decisively against it.

There are also a huge number of bilateral and multilateral trade agreements that China has signed in good faith and abides by.

## **5\. The Patterns**

Now that we've seen a range of treaties China is party to, we can look for patterns. What predicts stronger and weaker compliance?

It would be lovely if there were a nice regression that we could do that would predict compliance to the letter and the spirit of various treaties, and design based on that, but I think this would be misleading. 

There's evidence from the comparative literature on what kinds of treaties generally “work”. One meta-analysis found that:

- Including enforcement mechanisms within treaties led to increased effectiveness  
- Transparency, complaint, and oversight mechanisms were very marginally effective  
- Trade and finance treaties are the only ones that reliably work, the rest are all ineffective  
- Treaties with only a few countries work more effectively   
- Requiring a change in products is more effective than changes in people (), places , or policies ()  
- Economic outcomes work \- social and civil liberty outcomes don’t

![][image5]

Here's what I think the case studies tell us:

**Binary obligations survive better than scalar ones.** "Reduce CFC production to zero by 2010" is a binary obligation; you either did it or you didn't. "Progressively liberalise your economy" is scalar; there's always room to argue you're making progress. Despite scalar obligations being relatively common in Chinese domestic law, they're much harder to enforce in the international context because they give China room to comply with the letter while ignoring the spirit.

### **Enforcement channels matter**

**Centralised gatekeeping produces more compliance than distributed monitoring.** When compliance depends on a single checkpoint \- like IAEA certification being required before any nuclear technology transfer can happen \- China tends to comply. When compliance depends on monitoring thousands of individual actors across dozens of provinces, things break down. There's a chicken-and-egg quality to this: if you want someone to comply, make the mechanism centralised. But it also reflects how China's own system works; the central government can force outcomes when it chooses to, but has less control over what happens at the provincial level.

### **Incentives beat architecture**

**Commercial incentives and bilateral leverage work better than treaty design.** Access to technology (IAEA), access to markets (WTO tariff commitments), and access to supply chains (Montreal Protocol funding) are stronger motivators than elegant legal frameworks. China joined the IAEA regime because it wanted civilian nuclear technology. It reduced tariffs because it wanted market access. When the incentive is abstract \- "be a good global citizen" \- compliance is weaker.

### **The letter, not the spirit**

**China complies with the letter, not necessarily the spirit, of its obligations.** This is a consistent pattern across every case study. When an obligation has a clear, measurable threshold, China tends to meet it. When the obligation is about adopting a particular ethos or direction of travel \- market liberalisation, human rights protections, transparency norms \- China does the minimum that allows it to claim compliance.

### **Leadership matters more than you'd think**

**It depends on who's in power; not just at the time of signing.** Zhu Rongji's commitment to economic reform made WTO accession possible, but Xi Jinping's subsequent consolidation of state economic control hollowed out many of its commitments. Treaty designers need to think about whether obligations can survive leadership transitions.

### **Politics can derail everything**

Tiananmen, the Belgrade embassy bombing, nuclear espionage accusations; all of these disrupted treaty negotiations that were otherwise on track. It's fairly obvious that any crisis over Taiwan would put a stick in the spokes of any ongoing AI governance process.

**What Does This Mean for AI?**

I'll save the detailed application to AI governance for the next piece. 

If you're designing an international AI governance regime and you want China to comply, the case studies suggest you should:

* **Favour binary, measurable obligations** over vague aspirational ones  
* Favour administrative obligations over ones that require legislative change  
* **Build in centralised gatekeeping mechanisms** rather than relying on distributed monitoring  
* **Attach commercial incentives**  access to compute, chips, or markets rather than relying on good faith  
* **Anticipate leadership change** \- obligations should be hard to hollow out even when the political winds shift  
* **Accept that the spirit of the agreement will be tested** and build accordingly

And you should be realistic about what won't work: voluntary reporting, aspirational commitments, obligations that depend on transparency from a system that treats transparency as a vulnerability.

China isn't impossible to negotiate with. The CWC works. The IAEA works. Large parts of WTO compliance worked, at least for a while. But the treaties that work share specific structural features, and the ones that fail tend to fail for predictable reasons. Understanding those reasons seems like a prerequisite for anyone serious about AI governance.

*Next: applying this framework to specific AI governance proposals.*
